title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a theatre manager called Martin talking about a training programme for people interested in backstage work. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a theatre manager called Martin talking about a training programme for people interested in backstage work. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A theatre manager called Martin talks to people interested in backstage work.
extracts.audio_script: Good evening. I’m Martin, the manager at Lakeside Theatre, and I’d like to introduce our training programme for volunteers. Our building does not look like a traditional theatre from outside, and there is a reason for that. It was designed as a [9]cinema[/9] in the 1930s, then stood empty for several years before local performers raised money to convert it. The old ticket window remains at the entrance, although the seats and stage have been completely replaced. The theatre now presents drama, music and children’s performances throughout the year.||Many people applying to join us say they want to act, but the training programme is for the work audiences do not usually notice. It covers sound, costumes, stage management and scenery. The area where we most urgently need new helpers is [10]lighting[/10], since two experienced volunteers are moving away this summer. The course does not require any technical knowledge. It takes place for six weeks, and although Saturday afternoons were suggested, the group chose [11]Tuesday evenings[/11], because several members work at weekends. Each meeting lasts just under two hours, with plenty of practical work.||You should come prepared to move around backstage. Please do not buy special shoes or expensive equipment. Comfortable footwear is enough, but on show nights all backstage helpers need to wear [12]black clothing[/12], so they are not easily seen by the audience when they move scenery. During the course, each learner will help prepare part of a real production. Some expected to paint scenery, but this time you will contribute written information for the printed [13]programme[/13], interviewing a performer and checking details about the play. This gives you a useful understanding of how the whole production comes together.||In addition to the weekly training, you may select one optional session. We offered a talk about theatre history last year and only five people came, whereas costumes proved extremely popular. This season, the optional event will be a [14]make-up workshop[/14], led by a professional who has worked on television. As part of that session, you will visit the [15]costume store[/15], where dresses, hats and uniforms are kept; people are often surprised by how carefully these items must be labelled. Safety matters in a public building, so everyone must also take part in a [16]fire drill[/16] before assisting at a performance.||Volunteers gain experience rather than payment. We cannot promise employment, but after helping with three productions, you may request a written [17]reference[/17] for college or job applications. Your first chance to put the training into practice will be in July. We originally planned a musical, but it requires more singers than we have available. The summer production is now a [18]comedy show[/18], which needs a small cast but plenty of help behind the scenes. Application forms are available in the foyer. Please complete one before you leave if you would like to join us.

dialogue.speaker: martin
dialogue.text: Good evening. I’m Martin, the manager at Lakeside Theatre, and I’d like to introduce our training programme for volunteers. Our building does not look like a traditional theatre from outside, and there is a reason for that. It was designed as a cinema in the 1930s, then stood empty for several years before local performers raised money to convert it. The old ticket window remains at the entrance, although the seats and stage have been completely replaced. The theatre now presents drama, music and children’s performances throughout the year.||Many people applying to join us say they want to act, but the training programme is for the work audiences do not usually notice. It covers sound, costumes, stage management and scenery. The area where we most urgently need new helpers is lighting, since two experienced volunteers are moving away this summer. The course does not require any technical knowledge. It takes place for six weeks, and although Saturday afternoons were suggested, the group chose Tuesday evenings, because several members work at weekends. Each meeting lasts just under two hours, with plenty of practical work.||You should come prepared to move around backstage. Please do not buy special shoes or expensive equipment. Comfortable footwear is enough, but on show nights all backstage helpers need to wear black clothing, so they are not easily seen by the audience when they move scenery. During the course, each learner will help prepare part of a real production. Some expected to paint scenery, but this time you will contribute written information for the printed programme, interviewing a performer and checking details about the play. This gives you a useful understanding of how the whole production comes together.||In addition to the weekly training, you may select one optional session. We offered a talk about theatre history last year and only five people came, whereas costumes proved extremely popular. This season, the optional event will be a make-up workshop, led by a professional who has worked on television. As part of that session, you will visit the costume store, where dresses, hats and uniforms are kept; people are often surprised by how carefully these items must be labelled. Safety matters in a public building, so everyone must also take part in a fire drill before assisting at a performance.||Volunteers gain experience rather than payment. We cannot promise employment, but after helping with three productions, you may request a written reference for college or job applications. Your first chance to put the training into practice will be in July. We originally planned a musical, but it requires more singers than we have available. The summer production is now a comedy show, which needs a small cast but plenty of help behind the scenes. Application forms are available in the foyer. Please complete one before you leave if you would like to join us.

questions.question: The theatre building was originally designed as a (9) .
questions.explanation: Martin explains that the building was first designed as a 'cinema'.

questions.question: The theatre particularly needs volunteers to work on (10) .
questions.explanation: Martin identifies 'lighting' as the area with the greatest need for helpers.

questions.question: The six-week course will take place on (11) .
questions.explanation: Martin says the group selected 'Tuesday evenings'.

questions.question: Helpers backstage during performances are required to wear (12) .
questions.explanation: Martin states that backstage helpers must wear 'black clothing' on show nights.

questions.question: Learners will provide information for the printed (13) .
questions.explanation: The learners will help create written information for the printed 'programme'.

questions.question: The optional event this season will be a (14) .
questions.explanation: Martin announces a 'make-up workshop' as the optional event.

questions.question: Trainees will see where clothing for performances is kept in the (15) .
questions.explanation: Martin says the workshop includes a visit to the 'costume store'.

questions.question: Before helping at a performance, everyone must join a (16) .
questions.explanation: Martin states that all helpers must participate in a 'fire drill'.

questions.question: After working on three productions, a volunteer can ask for a written (17) .
questions.explanation: Martin offers a written 'reference' after three productions.

questions.question: The first summer production trainees can help with is a (18) .
questions.explanation: Martin explains that the production has changed to a 'comedy show'.
